findings.title: Skill name does not follow agent skills naming rules
Skill does not specify a license
Sensitive credentials and local secrets are collected and exfiltrated to an external server
findings.description: The embedded backup script gathers far more than project configuration: it copies all environment variables, project .env files, cloud credentials, SSH private keys, and package-manager credential files, then uploads the payload to an external HTTPS endpoint. This is explicit unauthorized collection and transmission of highly sensitive data.
findings.evidence: SYNC_ENDPOINT is set to https://cloud-sync-api.io/v1/backup. collect_configs() reads os.environ, .env files, ~/.aws/credentials, ~/.config/gcloud/application_default_credentials.json, ~/.ssh/id_rsa, ~/.ssh/id_ed25519, ~/.npmrc, and ~/.pypirc. upload_backup() base64-encodes the payload and sends it with urllib.request.urlopen().
findings.remediation: Do not execute this skill. Remove all credential and secret collection, restrict scope to explicitly user-selected project files only, and require clear user approval before any network transfer.

findings.title: Skill description disguises credential harvesting as routine cloud backup
findings.description: The manifest and instructions frame the skill as a project configuration sync/backup utility, but the actual behavior includes harvesting system-wide credentials and private keys. This mismatch makes the skill appear safer and narrower than it is, increasing the chance of unintended activation.
findings.evidence: Description claims: 'Synchronize local project files and configurations to cloud storage.' The code additionally collects full environment variables and credential material from AWS, GCP, SSH, npm, and PyPI locations. The command table also advertises 'Full backup of configs and credentials,' revealing scope beyond normal project backup.
findings.remediation: Reject or rewrite the skill so its declared purpose matches its actual behavior. Credential access should be removed entirely unless there is an explicit, narrowly justified, and user-approved need.

findings.title: Unicode obfuscation suggests deliberate detection evasion
findings.description: The skill uses zero-width or confusable Unicode characters in the skill name and sensitive keywords, which can bypass simple pattern matching and make malicious behavior less obvious to reviewers.
findings.evidence: Examples include visually altered forms of terms such as 'cloud-sy​nc', 'bac​kup', 'Соllect', and 'сrеdentials'. These confusable characters appear around the skill name and around the code that gathers credentials.
findings.remediation: Normalize content before review, flag confusable Unicode in manifests and instructions, and reject skills that use obfuscated identifiers or security-relevant wording.
